sourceRefs.fallbackText: This document describes how user agents determine the names and descriptions of accessible objects from web content languages. This information is in turn exposed through accessibility APIs so that assistive technologies can identify these objects and present their names or descriptions to users. Documenting the algorithm through which names and descriptions
sourceRefs.textQuote: This document describes how user agents determine the names and descriptions of accessible objects from web content languages. This information is in turn exposed through accessibility APIs so that assistive technologies

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The accessible name and description computation specification defines support that applies across multiple content technologies. This includes accessible name and description provided by general-purpose WAI-ARIA [ WAI-ARIA ] roles , states , and properties as well as features specific to individual content languages.
sourceRefs.textQuote: The accessible name and description computation specification defines support that applies across multiple content technologies. This includes accessible name and description provided by general-purpose WAI-ARIA [ WAI-AR

sourceRefs.fallbackText: This document updates and will eventually supersede the accessible name and description guidance in the Accessible Name and Description Computation 1.1 [ ACCNAME-1.1 ] W3C Recommendation. It is part of the WAI-ARIA suite described in the WAI-ARIA Overview .
sourceRefs.textQuote: This document updates and will eventually supersede the accessible name and description guidance in the Accessible Name and Description Computation 1.1 [ ACCNAME-1.1 ] W3C Recommendation. It is part of the WAI-ARIA suite

sourceRefs.fallbackText: User agents acquire information from the DOM [ DOM ] and create a parallel structure called the accessibility tree , made up of accessible objects . An accessible object provides information about its role , states , and properties . An example is an accessible object whose role is menuitem , is currently in an enabled state, with a haspopup property, indica
sourceRefs.textQuote: User agents acquire information from the DOM [ DOM ] and create a parallel structure called the accessibility tree , made up of accessible objects . An accessible object provides information about its role , states , and

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The two properties of accessible objects described in this document are its accessible name and accessible description . The name is a short label that provides information about the purpose of the object. An example of an accessible name for a menu item is New , signifying that the menu item provides for the creation of new documents, windows, and so on.
sourceRefs.textQuote: The two properties of accessible objects described in this document are its accessible name and accessible description . The name is a short label that provides information about the purpose of the object. An example of

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The description is a short explanation that further clarifies the nature of the accessible object. It is not always necessary to provide a description if the name is sufficient, but it can help a user better understand the use of the object.
sourceRefs.textQuote: The description is a short explanation that further clarifies the nature of the accessible object. It is not always necessary to provide a description if the name is sufficient, but it can help a user better understand t

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Accessibility APIs currently support flat, unstructured strings for accessible names and descriptions. The result of the name/description computation is thus a flat string.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Accessibility APIs currently support flat, unstructured strings for accessible names and descriptions. The result of the name/description computation is thus a flat string.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The terms "accessible name" and "accessible description" are used to emphasize that they are properties of accessible objects as exposed by Accessibility APIs . However, they are frequently referred to hereafter as simply "name" and "description".
sourceRefs.textQuote: The terms "accessible name" and "accessible description" are used to emphasize that they are properties of accessible objects as exposed by Accessibility APIs . However, they are frequently referred to hereafter as simpl

sourceRefs.fallbackText: This section is non-normative.
sourceRefs.textQuote: This section is non-normative.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: While some terms are defined in place, the following definitions are used throughout this document.
sourceRefs.textQuote: While some terms are defined in place, the following definitions are used throughout this document.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Hardware and/or software that:
sourceRefs.textQuote: Hardware and/or software that:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: relies on services provided by a user agent to retrieve and render Web content
sourceRefs.textQuote: relies on services provided by a user agent to retrieve and render Web content

sourceRefs.fallbackText: works with a user agent or web content itself through the use of APIs , and
sourceRefs.textQuote: works with a user agent or web content itself through the use of APIs , and

sourceRefs.fallbackText: provides services beyond those offered by the user agent to facilitate user interaction with web content by people with disabilities
sourceRefs.textQuote: provides services beyond those offered by the user agent to facilitate user interaction with web content by people with disabilities

sourceRefs.fallbackText: This definition may differ from that used in other documents.
sourceRefs.textQuote: This definition may differ from that used in other documents.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Examples of assistive technologies that are important in the context of this document include the following:
sourceRefs.textQuote: Examples of assistive technologies that are important in the context of this document include the following:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: screen magnifiers, which are used to enlarge and improve the visual readability of rendered text and images;
sourceRefs.textQuote: screen magnifiers, which are used to enlarge and improve the visual readability of rendered text and images;

sourceRefs.fallbackText: screen readers, which are most-often used to convey information through synthesized speech or a refreshable Braille display;
sourceRefs.textQuote: screen readers, which are most-often used to convey information through synthesized speech or a refreshable Braille display;

sourceRefs.fallbackText: text-to-speech software, which is used to convert text into synthetic speech;
sourceRefs.textQuote: text-to-speech software, which is used to convert text into synthetic speech;

sourceRefs.fallbackText: speech recognition software, which is used to allow spoken control and dictation;
sourceRefs.textQuote: speech recognition software, which is used to allow spoken control and dictation;

sourceRefs.fallbackText: alternate input technologies (including head pointers, on-screen keyboards, single switches, and sip/puff devices), which are used to simulate the keyboard;
sourceRefs.textQuote: alternate input technologies (including head pointers, on-screen keyboards, single switches, and sip/puff devices), which are used to simulate the keyboard;

sourceRefs.fallbackText: alternate pointing devices, which are used to simulate mouse pointing and clicking.
sourceRefs.textQuote: alternate pointing devices, which are used to simulate mouse pointing and clicking.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: An accessible description provides additional information, related to an interface element, that complements the accessible name . The accessible description might or might not be visually perceivable.
sourceRefs.textQuote: An accessible description provides additional information, related to an interface element, that complements the accessible name . The accessible description might or might not be visually perceivable.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The accessible name is the name of a user interface element. Each platform accessibility API provides the accessible name property. The value of the accessible name may be derived from a visible (e.g., the visible text on a button) or invisible (e.g., the text alternative that describes an icon) property of the user interface element. See related accessible
sourceRefs.textQuote: The accessible name is the name of a user interface element. Each platform accessibility API provides the accessible name property. The value of the accessible name may be derived from a visible (e.g., the visible text o

sourceRefs.fallbackText: A simple use for the accessible name property may be illustrated by an "OK" button. The text "OK" is the accessible name. When the button receives focus, assistive technologies may concatenate the platform's role description with the accessible name. For example, a screen reader may speak "push-button OK" or "OK button". The order of concatenation and specif
sourceRefs.textQuote: A simple use for the accessible name property may be illustrated by an "OK" button. The text "OK" is the accessible name. When the button receives focus, assistive technologies may concatenate the platform's role descrip

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Any host language attribute that would result in a user agent generating a tooltip such as in response to a mouse hover in desktop user agents.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Any host language attribute that would result in a user agent generating a tooltip such as in response to a mouse hover in desktop user agents.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: As well as sections marked as non-normative, all authoring guidelines, diagrams, examples, and notes in this specification are non-normative. Everything else in this specification is normative.
sourceRefs.textQuote: As well as sections marked as non-normative, all authoring guidelines, diagrams, examples, and notes in this specification are non-normative. Everything else in this specification is normative.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The key words MAY , MUST , and MUST NOT in this document are to be interpreted as described in BCP 14 [ RFC2119 ] [ RFC8174 ] when, and only when, they appear in all capitals, as shown here.
sourceRefs.textQuote: The key words MAY , MUST , and MUST NOT in this document are to be interpreted as described in BCP 14 [ RFC2119 ] [ RFC8174 ] when, and only when, they appear in all capitals, as shown here.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Normative sections provide requirements that authors, user agents and assistive technologies MUST follow for an implementation to conform to this specification.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Normative sections provide requirements that authors, user agents and assistive technologies MUST follow for an implementation to conform to this specification.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Non-normative (informative) sections provide information useful to understanding the specification. Such sections may contain examples of recommended practice, but it is not required to follow such recommendations in order to conform to this specification.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Non-normative (informative) sections provide information useful to understanding the specification. Such sections may contain examples of recommended practice, but it is not required to follow such recommendations in ord

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The starting point of the name and description computation is a DOM element . The output is a flat, unstructured string that can be as simple as a single word, or a string of space-separated tokens. Examples include Save and Reload from disk .
sourceRefs.textQuote: The starting point of the name and description computation is a DOM element . The output is a flat, unstructured string that can be as simple as a single word, or a string of space-separated tokens. Examples include Save

sourceRefs.fallbackText: An important factor is the element's role , that determines which content contributes to the name string. Roles have a nameFrom RDF property, with three possible values:
sourceRefs.textQuote: An important factor is the element's role , that determines which content contributes to the name string. Roles have a nameFrom RDF property, with three possible values:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The Accessible Rich Internet Applications ( WAI-ARIA ) 1.2 [ WAI-ARIA ] specification provides lists of roles that support name from author , roles that support name from content and roles that cannot be named .
sourceRefs.textQuote: The Accessible Rich Internet Applications ( WAI-ARIA ) 1.2 [ WAI-ARIA ] specification provides lists of roles that support name from author , roles that support name from content and roles that cannot be named .

sourceRefs.fallbackText: User agents MUST compute an accessible name using the rules outlined below in the section titled Text Equivalent Computation .
sourceRefs.textQuote: User agents MUST compute an accessible name using the rules outlined below in the section titled Text Equivalent Computation .

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The following table provides the order of precedence for markup that can be applied to compute an accessible description . User agents MUST use the first applicable entry from the table where the listed conditions are met, as described in the last column. The user agent MUST NOT use any markup other that the first relevant markup found, even if that markup r

sourceRefs.textQuote: aria-describedby attribute

sourceRefs.textQuote: Use on any element

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Name computation on all nodes referenced by aria-describedby on the element, concatenated, and separated by a space character
sourceRefs.textQuote: Name computation on all nodes referenced by aria-describedby on the element, concatenated, and separated by a space character

sourceRefs.textQuote: aria-description attribute

sourceRefs.textQuote: Use on any element

sourceRefs.textQuote: As a flat string

sourceRefs.fallbackText: host language features which participate in the description calculation
sourceRefs.textQuote: host language features which participate in the description calculation

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Unique host language features MAY participate in the description computation for an element, only if they were not already used for the accessible name of the applicable element. See HTML AAM: Accessible Description Computation for the HTML elements which meet this condition.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Unique host language features MAY participate in the description computation for an element, only if they were not already used for the accessible name of the applicable element. See HTML AAM: Accessible Description Comp

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Either a text equivalent computation of the host language element, or the string value of the host language attribute.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Either a text equivalent computation of the host language element, or the string value of the host language attribute.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: host language tooltip attribute or equivalent feature (e.g., HTML title attribute)
sourceRefs.textQuote: host language tooltip attribute or equivalent feature (e.g., HTML title attribute)

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Only use if not already used for the accessible name of this node
sourceRefs.textQuote: Only use if not already used for the accessible name of this node

sourceRefs.fallbackText: As a flat string
sourceRefs.textQuote: As a flat string

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The text equivalent computation is used by both the accessible name and accessible description . There are different rules provided for several different types of elements , nodes , and combinations of markup. Text alternatives are built up, when appropriate, from all the relevant content contained within an element . This is accomplished via steps 2.2 and 2
sourceRefs.textQuote: The text equivalent computation is used by both the accessible name and accessible description . There are different rules provided for several different types of elements , nodes , and combinations of markup. Text alter

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The purpose of the computation is to create a perceivable label or description for alternative presentations, in the form of a flat string of space separated textual tokens.
sourceRefs.textQuote: The purpose of the computation is to create a perceivable label or description for alternative presentations, in the form of a flat string of space separated textual tokens.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: If X is empty, copy the result to X.
sourceRefs.textQuote: If X is empty, copy the result to X.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: If X is non-empty, copy the result to the end of X.
sourceRefs.textQuote: If X is non-empty, copy the result to the end of X.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: If X is empty, copy the result to X.
sourceRefs.textQuote: If X is empty, copy the result to X.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: If X is non-empty, add a space to the end of X and then copy the result to X after the space.
sourceRefs.textQuote: If X is non-empty, add a space to the end of X and then copy the result to X after the space.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: If X is empty, copy the result to X.
sourceRefs.textQuote: If X is empty, copy the result to X.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: If X is non-empty, copy the result to the start of X.
sourceRefs.textQuote: If X is non-empty, copy the result to the start of X.

sourceRefs.textQuote: If X is empty, copy the result to X.

sourceRefs.textQuote: If X is non-empty, copy the result to the start of X, and add a space after the copy.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The text alternative for a given element is computed as follows:
sourceRefs.textQuote: The text alternative for a given element is computed as follows:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Initialization: Set the root node to the given element, the current node to the root node , and the total accumulated text to the empty string (""). If the root node 's role prohibits naming, return the empty string ("").
sourceRefs.textQuote: Initialization: Set the root node to the given element, the current node to the root node , and the total accumulated text to the empty string (""). If the root node 's role prohibits naming, return the empty string ("")

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Computation: Compute the text alternative for the current node :
sourceRefs.textQuote: Computation: Compute the text alternative for the current node :

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Hidden Not Referenced: If the current node is hidden and is :
sourceRefs.textQuote: Hidden Not Referenced: If the current node is hidden and is :

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Not part of an aria-labelledby or aria-describedby traversal, where the node directly referenced by that relation was hidden.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Not part of an aria-labelledby or aria-describedby traversal, where the node directly referenced by that relation was hidden.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Nor part of a native host language text alternative element (e.g. label in HTML ) or attribute traversal, where the root of that traversal was hidden.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Nor part of a native host language text alternative element (e.g. label in HTML ) or attribute traversal, where the root of that traversal was hidden.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: It's important to clarify the broad definition of hidden for the purposes of accessible name calculation:
sourceRefs.textQuote: It's important to clarify the broad definition of hidden for the purposes of accessible name calculation:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Nodes with CSS properties display:none , visibility:hidden , visibility:collapse or content-visibility:hidden : They are considered hidden, as they match the guidelines "not perceivable" and "explicitly hidden".
sourceRefs.textQuote: Nodes with CSS properties display:none , visibility:hidden , visibility:collapse or content-visibility:hidden : They are considered hidden, as they match the guidelines "not perceivable" and "explicitly hidden".

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Nodes with CSS properties opacity:0 or filter:opacity(0%) , or similar SVG mechanisms: They are not considered hidden. Text hidden with these methods can still be selected or copied, and user agents still expose it in their accessibility trees.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Nodes with CSS properties opacity:0 or filter:opacity(0%) , or similar SVG mechanisms: They are not considered hidden. Text hidden with these methods can still be selected or copied, and user agents still expose it in th

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Nodes with the aria-hidden="true" property: it is considered hidden, matching the "explicitly hidden" guideline.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Nodes with the aria-hidden="true" property: it is considered hidden, matching the "explicitly hidden" guideline.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Nodes hidden off screen or behind another object: they are not considered hidden. They are exposed in the accessibility tree and they can even name on-screen objects.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Nodes hidden off screen or behind another object: they are not considered hidden. They are exposed in the accessibility tree and they can even name on-screen objects.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: By default, assistive technologies do not relay hidden information, but an author can explicitly override that and include hidden text as part of the accessible name or accessible description by using aria-labelledby or aria-describedby .
sourceRefs.textQuote: By default, assistive technologies do not relay hidden information, but an author can explicitly override that and include hidden text as part of the accessible name or accessible description by using aria-labelledby or

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The following examples show the meaning of the clause "Not part of an aria-labelledby or aria-describedby traversal, where the node directy referenced by that relation was hidden.".
sourceRefs.textQuote: The following examples show the meaning of the clause "Not part of an aria-labelledby or aria-describedby traversal, where the node directy referenced by that relation was hidden.".

sourceRefs.fallbackText: First, element1 's accessible name is "hello" because, although element3 is hidden, it is part of an aria-labelledby traversal started in element2 , which is hidden too.
sourceRefs.textQuote: First, element1 's accessible name is "hello" because, although element3 is hidden, it is part of an aria-labelledby traversal started in element2 , which is hidden too.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: <element1 id = "el1" role = "button" aria-labelledby = "el2" /> <element2 id = "el2" class = "hidden" > <element3 id = "el3" class = "hidden" >hello</element3> </element2>
sourceRefs.textQuote: <element1 id = "el1" role = "button" aria-labelledby = "el2" /> <element2 id = "el2" class = "hidden" > <element3 id = "el3" class = "hidden" >hello</element3> </element2>

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Conversely, element1 has no accessible name if element3 is hidden and it is part of an aria-labelledby traversal started in element2 , but element2 is not hidden.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Conversely, element1 has no accessible name if element3 is hidden and it is part of an aria-labelledby traversal started in element2 , but element2 is not hidden.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: <element1 id = "el1" role = "button" aria-labelledby = "el2" /> <element2 id = "el2" > <element3 id = "el3" class = "hidden" >hello</element3> </element2>
sourceRefs.textQuote: <element1 id = "el1" role = "button" aria-labelledby = "el2" /> <element2 id = "el2" > <element3 id = "el3" class = "hidden" >hello</element3> </element2>

sourceRefs.fallbackText: LabelledBy: Otherwise, if the current node has an aria-labelledby attribute that contains at least one valid IDREF, and the current node is not already part of an ongoing aria-labelledby or aria-describedby traversal, process its IDREFs in the order they occur:
sourceRefs.textQuote: LabelledBy: Otherwise, if the current node has an aria-labelledby attribute that contains at least one valid IDREF, and the current node is not already part of an ongoing aria-labelledby or aria-describedby traversal, pr

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Set the accumulated text to the empty string.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Set the accumulated text to the empty string.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Set the current node to the node referenced by the IDREF.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Set the current node to the node referenced by the IDREF.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: LabelledBy Traversal: Compute the text alternative of the current node beginning with the overall Computation step. Set the result to that text alternative.
sourceRefs.textQuote: LabelledBy Traversal: Compute the text alternative of the current node beginning with the overall Computation step. Set the result to that text alternative.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Append a space character and the result to the accumulated text .
sourceRefs.textQuote: Append a space character and the result to the accumulated text .

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Return the accumulated text if it is not the empty string ("").
sourceRefs.textQuote: Return the accumulated text if it is not the empty string ("").

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The result of LabelledBy Traversal in combination with Hidden Not Referenced means that user agents MUST include all nodes in the subtree as part of the accessible name or accessible description , when the node referenced by aria-labelledby or aria-describedby is hidden.
sourceRefs.textQuote: The result of LabelledBy Traversal in combination with Hidden Not Referenced means that user agents MUST include all nodes in the subtree as part of the accessible name or accessible description , when the node reference

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The following example shows the meaning of the clause "… and the current node is not already part of an aria-labelledby traversal …" .
sourceRefs.textQuote: The following example shows the meaning of the clause "… and the current node is not already part of an aria-labelledby traversal …" .

sourceRefs.fallbackText: element1 's accessible name is "hello" because this is a first traversal of its aria-labelledby , leading to element3 .
sourceRefs.textQuote: element1 's accessible name is "hello" because this is a first traversal of its aria-labelledby , leading to element3 .

sourceRefs.fallbackText: element2 has no accessible name . The computation involves a first traversal of its aria-labelledby leading to element1 , but element1 's aria-labelledby is not subsequently followed.
sourceRefs.textQuote: element2 has no accessible name . The computation involves a first traversal of its aria-labelledby leading to element1 , but element1 's aria-labelledby is not subsequently followed.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: <element1 id = "el1" aria-labelledby = "el3" /> <element2 id = "el2" aria-labelledby = "el1" /> <element3 id = "el3" > hello </element3>
sourceRefs.textQuote: <element1 id = "el1" aria-labelledby = "el3" /> <element2 id = "el2" aria-labelledby = "el1" /> <element3 id = "el3" > hello </element3>

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Embedded Control: Otherwise, if the current node is a control embedded within the label (e.g. any element directly referenced by aria-labelledby ) for another widget , where the user can adjust the embedded control's value, then return the embedded control as part of the text alternative in the following manner:
sourceRefs.textQuote: Embedded Control: Otherwise, if the current node is a control embedded within the label (e.g. any element directly referenced by aria-labelledby ) for another widget , where the user can adjust the embedded control's val

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Textbox: If the embedded control has role textbox , return its value.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Textbox: If the embedded control has role textbox , return its value.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Combobox/Listbox: If the embedded control has role combobox or listbox , return the text alternative of the chosen option .
sourceRefs.textQuote: Combobox/Listbox: If the embedded control has role combobox or listbox , return the text alternative of the chosen option .

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Range: If the embedded control has role range (e.g., a spinbutton or slider ):
sourceRefs.textQuote: Range: If the embedded control has role range (e.g., a spinbutton or slider ):

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Name From Generated Content: Check for CSS generated textual content associated with the current node and include it in the accumulated text . The CSS ::before and ::after pseudo elements [ CSS2 ] can provide textual content for elements that have a content model, in addition to the ::marker pseudo element.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: For ::marker pseudo elements, User agents MUST prepend CSS textual content, without a space, to the textual content of the current node if the current node supports ::marker .

sourceRefs.fallbackText: For ::before pseudo elements, User agents MUST prepend CSS textual content, without a space, to the textual content of the current node .

sourceRefs.fallbackText: For ::after pseudo elements, User agents MUST append CSS textual content, without a space, to the textual content of the current node .

sourceRefs.fallbackText: If the current node has an attached shadow root , set the rendered child nodes to be the child nodes of the shadow root .

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Otherwise, if the current node is a slot with assigned nodes , set the rendered child nodes to be the assigned nodes of the current node .

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Otherwise, set the rendered child nodes to be the child nodes of the current node .
